749 pounds (340 kg) (with breech) * **Mark 13**: 531 pounds (241 kg) * **Mark 14 Mod 0**: 593 pounds (269 kg) (with breech) * **Mark 14 Mod 1**: 658 pounds (298 kg) (with breech) |

(spoken "three-inch-twenty-three-caliber") was the standard [anti-aircraft](/wiki/Anti-aircraft "Anti-aircraft") gun for United States [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyers "Destroyers") through [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and the 1920s. United States naval gun terminology indicates the gun fired a projectile 3 inches (76 mm) in diameter, and the barrel was 23 [calibers](/wiki/Caliber_(artillery) "Caliber (artillery)") long (barrel length is 3" × 23 = 69" or 1.75 meters.)[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [3"/23 caliber gun](/w/index.php?title=3%22/23_caliber_gun&redirect=no "3\"/23 caliber gun"))

US Navy anti-aircraft gun

| [Elevation](/wiki/Elevation_(ballistics) "Elevation (ballistics)") | -15° to +65° or +75° |

The **3-inch/23-caliber gun** (spoken "three-inch-twenty-three-caliber") was the standard [anti-aircraft](/wiki/Anti-aircraft "Anti-aircraft") gun for United States [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyers "Destroyers") through [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and the 1920s. United States naval gun terminology indicates the gun fired a projectile 3 inches (76 mm) in diameter, and the barrel was 23 [calibers](/wiki/Caliber_(artillery) "Caliber (artillery)") long (barrel length is 3" × 23 = 69" or 1.75 meters.)[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=3-inch/23-caliber_gun&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/3_23_caliber_gun_Mk14_mod11.jpg/250px-3_23_caliber_gun_Mk14_mod11.jpg)](/wiki/File:3_23_caliber_gun_Mk14_mod11.jpg)

Plan and left elevation diagrams

The [built-up gun](/wiki/Built-up_gun "Built-up gun") with horizontal [sliding breech](/wiki/Rifled_breech_loader#The_sliding_block "Rifled breech loader") block weighed about 531 pounds (241 kg) and used fixed ammunition (case and projectile handled as a single assembled unit) with a 13-pound (6 kg) projectile at a velocity of 1650 feet per second (500 m/s).[[2]](#cite_note-Campbell_1985_p.146-2) Range was 10,100 yards (9235 meters) at 45 degrees elevation.[[2]](#cite_note-Campbell_1985_p.146-2) Ceiling was 18,000 feet (5500 meters) at the maximum elevation of 75 degrees.[[2]](#cite_note-Campbell_1985_p.146-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=3-inch/23-caliber_gun&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The 3"/23-caliber cannon was the first purposely designed [anti-aircraft](/wiki/Anti-aircraft "Anti-aircraft") cannon to reach operational service in the US military, and was a further development of a 1-pounder cannon concept designed by [Admiral Twining](/wiki/Nathan_Crook_Twining "Nathan Crook Twining") to meet the possible threat from airships being built by various navies.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It entered service in late 1913 on the *Aylwin*-class destroyers.

A partially retractable version was mounted as a [deck gun](/wiki/Deck_gun "Deck gun") on the US [L-class](/wiki/United_States_L-class_submarine "United States L-class submarine"), [*M-1* (SS-47)](/wiki/USS_M-1 "USS M-1"), [*AA-1* class](/wiki/AA-1-class_submarine "AA-1-class submarine"), and [O-class](/wiki/United_States_O-class_submarine "United States O-class submarine") [submarines](/wiki/Submarines "Submarines").

When [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") began, the 3"/23-caliber gun was outdated, and surviving United States destroyers built during the [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") era that were armed with the 3"/23-caliber were rearmed with dual-purpose [3"/50-caliber guns](/wiki/3%22/50-caliber_gun "3\"/50-caliber gun"). Where there was no air threat during World War II, the 3"/23-caliber gun was employed in the surface-to-surface role for use against submarines, and was mounted on submarine chasers, armed yachts, and various auxiliaries.[[2]](#cite_note-Campbell_1985_p.146-2) Some major warships carried 3"/23-caliber guns temporarily while awaiting installation of quad [1.1"/75-caliber guns](/wiki/1.1%22/75_caliber_gun "1.1\"/75 caliber gun").[[2]](#cite_note-Campbell_1985_p.146-2)

The 3"/23-caliber gun was mounted on:

* [*AA-1*-class submarines](/wiki/AA-1-class_submarine "AA-1-class submarine")
* [*Active*-class patrol boats](/wiki/Active-class_patrol_boat "Active-class patrol boat")
* [*Aylwin*-class destroyers](/wiki/Aylwin-class_destroyer "Aylwin-class destroyer")
* [*Caldwell*-class destroyers](/wiki/Caldwell-class_destroyer "Caldwell-class destroyer")[[4]](#cite_note-Fahey_1939_p.14-4)
* [*Colorado*-class battleships](/wiki/Colorado-class_battleship "Colorado-class battleship")
* [*Clemson*-class destroyers](/wiki/Clemson-class_destroyer "Clemson-class destroyer")[[4]](#cite_note-Fahey_1939_p.14-4)
* [*Dubuque-class gunboats*](/wiki/Dubuque-class_gunboat "Dubuque-class gunboat")
* [*Florida*-class battleships](/wiki/Florida-class_battleship "Florida-class battleship")
* [*Klondike*-class destroyer tenders](/wiki/Klondike-class_destroyer_tender "Klondike-class destroyer tender")
* [*L-class submarines*](/wiki/United_States_L-class_submarine "United States L-class submarine")
* [USS *M-1* (SS-47)](/wiki/USS_M-1 "USS M-1")
* [*O-class submarines*](/wiki/United_States_O-class_submarine "United States O-class submarine")
* [*PGM-1*-class motor gunboat](/wiki/PGM-1-class_motor_gunboat "PGM-1-class motor gunboat")
* [*SC-1*-class submarine chasers](/wiki/SC-1-class_submarine_chaser "SC-1-class submarine chaser")
* [*SC-497*-class submarine chasers](/wiki/SC-497-class_submarine_chaser "SC-497-class submarine chaser")
* [*Wickes*-class destroyers](/wiki/Wickes-class_destroyer "Wickes-class destroyer")[[4]](#cite_note-Fahey_1939_p.14-4)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=3-inch/23-caliber_gun&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notes")]

3. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** ["New American Aerial Weapons"](https://books.google.com/books?id=-t0DAAAAMBAJ&dq=Popular+Mechanics+Science+installing+linoleum&pg=PA776) *Popular Mechanics*, December 1911, p. 776.
